insects.title: The Honeybee: Nature's Master Builder
insects.blurb: The honeybee builds cities of wax, communicates through dance, and keeps our food supply alive — all without a single day off.
paragraphs: The honeybee is one of the most important animals on Earth. Famous for producing honey and for their distinctive buzzing among flowers, bees have been part of human life for thousands of years: ancient Egyptians kept hives, cave paintings show people collecting honey, and today our food supply depends on them more than ever. Bees carry pollen between flowers as they feed, fertilising plants so they can produce fruit and seeds. Without bees doing this job, many of our most important crops — including apples, almonds, and strawberries — would simply fail to grow.

Bees evolved from wasp-like ancestors around 130 million years ago, at almost exactly the same time that flowering plants first appeared on Earth. This timing was no coincidence: flowers and bees evolved alongside one another over millions of years. Flowers developed nectar and vibrant colours — bold, intense, and eye-catching — to attract bees, while bees developed hairy bodies that trap pollen and mouths shaped to drink nectar efficiently. This partnership between bee and flower is one of the most successful relationships in the history of life, shaping landscapes and ecosystems across the whole planet.

A honeybee colony is one of the most remarkable examples of collective society — shared, cooperative, and deeply organised — in the animal kingdom. A single hive may contain up to 60,000 bees, all working together as one unit. The colony is structured around a strict hierarchy — a ranked order in which each bee has a fixed role. At the top sits the queen, the only bee that lays eggs. Below her are thousands of sterile female workers, who do every other job. A small group of male drones exist solely to mate with new queens, after which they die. Every individual serves the whole.

Worker bees are diligent beyond comparison — hardworking, tireless, and committed — performing an extraordinary range of tasks over their short lives. Young workers clean cells and feed larvae. As they age, they build honeycomb using wax they produce from glands in their own bodies, forming perfect hexagons that are mathematically the most efficient shape for storing honey without wasting material. Older workers forage for nectar and pollen, sometimes flying kilometres from the hive. At any moment the hive is teeming — buzzing and alive with constant movement — as thousands of bees work simultaneously, never stopping.

The honeybee's most ingenious ability — clever, inventive, and quite astonishing — is the waggle dance. When a forager discovers a rich patch of flowers, she returns to the hive and performs a precise figure-of-eight movement on the surface of the honeycomb. The angle of the dance tells her nestmates which direction to fly relative to the sun. The duration of the straight run tells them how far away the flowers are. This symbolic communication — describing a place that isn't present — was once thought to belong only to humans. In a creature with a brain the size of a sesame seed, it is a remarkable feat.

Honeybee populations have declined sharply in many parts of the world due to pesticides, disease, and the loss of wildflower meadows. This matters greatly because bees are essential for producing an abundant harvest — a plentiful and overflowing yield — from many important crops. Yet the honeybee has proved resilient — tough, enduring, and adaptable — across millions of years of changing climates and habitats. Planting wildflowers, reducing pesticide use, and supporting beekeepers are all making a real difference. The honeybee is tiny, but its place in the web of life is enormous.

insects.title: The Ant: The World's Most Successful Animal?
insects.blurb: There may be 20 quadrillion ants alive right now — more than all wild birds and mammals combined — and they have been reshaping the planet for 140 million years.
paragraphs: Ants are among the most successful animals ever to have lived on Earth. There are over 20,000 known species, found on every continent except Antarctica, in habitats from tropical rainforest to scorching desert. Scientists estimate there are approximately 20 quadrillion ants alive at this moment — a number so vast that it exceeds the number of stars visible in the night sky. Together, these ants weigh more than all the wild birds and wild mammals on the planet combined. For over 140 million years, they have been reshaping soil, forests, and food chains across every corner of the world.

Ants evolved from wasp-like ancestors during the Cretaceous period, around 140 to 168 million years ago, sharing the world with the last dinosaurs. The earliest ants were small ground-level predators. Over millions of years, different lineages developed increasingly sophisticated collective behaviours — ways in which the whole group operated together as a single coordinated unit — that gave them enormous advantages over solitary insects. By the time the dinosaurs went extinct, ants were already thriving on every continent, organised into colonies of staggering complexity that no other insect has come close to matching.

Every ant colony operates around a rigid hierarchy — a strict ranking in which each individual has a clearly defined role. At the top sits the queen, who may live for decades and lay millions of eggs over her lifetime. Worker ants — all female — carry out every other task: building the nest, gathering food, caring for the young, and defending the colony. The colony functions as a collective organism — a single coordinated whole made of thousands of individual bodies — so perfectly unified that biologists sometimes call it a 'superorganism,' a living system that is greater than the sum of its parts.

The daily work of an ant colony is arduous — difficult, demanding, and relentless — carried out without hesitation by millions of workers simultaneously. Leaf-cutter ants slice pieces of leaf far heavier than themselves and carry them for hundreds of metres to their underground fungus gardens. Army ants travel in enormous swarms, voraciously consuming — eagerly devouring in vast quantities — every insect and small creature they encounter. Some ant species tend aphids like tiny livestock, protecting them from predators in exchange for the sweet liquid the aphids produce. No resource is ignored; no opportunity is missed.

What makes ants so extraordinarily effective is that they are methodical — careful, systematic, and precise — in everything they do. Foragers lay down chemical trails called pheromones to direct nestmates to food. When a route is blocked, they find another, persistent and determined — refusing to stop until the problem is solved. Desert ants navigate across featureless sand by counting their own footsteps. Carpenter ants excavate wood with surgical accuracy. Army ants link their own bodies together to form bridges and rafts. Every species has its own precise set of tools and techniques, refined over millions of generations.

Ants are tenacious survivors — tough, adaptable, and seemingly unstoppable — that have outlasted five mass extinction events and colonised virtually every environment on Earth. They aerate soil, recycle nutrients, disperse seeds, and control the populations of other insects. When fire ants were accidentally introduced to North America from South America in the twentieth century, their aggressive, tenacious colonisation showed just how formidable a determined ant colony can be when released from its natural limits. The humble ant, marching in its endless columns, remains one of the great unseen forces shaping the natural world.

insects.title: The Dragonfly: 300 Million Years of Perfection
insects.blurb: Before birds existed, before dinosaurs roamed the Earth, dragonflies were already ruling the skies — and their hunting technique is still the most accurate of any animal alive.
paragraphs: The dragonfly is one of the oldest and most spectacular insects in the world. With wings that shimmer like stained glass and the ability to fly forwards, backwards, sideways, and hover perfectly still, dragonflies are among the most skilled aerial hunters the planet has ever produced. They have dazzled observers for millions of years: ancient fossils show that dragonfly-like insects once flew over swamps where dinosaurs drank, and some prehistoric relatives had wingspans as wide as an eagle. Today's dragonflies are smaller, but no less extraordinary in their design or their abilities.

Dragonflies are among the most venerable creatures alive today — ancient, long-established, and carrying an unbroken lineage stretching back over 300 million years. Long before birds, bats, or any other flying vertebrate existed, dragonflies were already masters of the air. A fossilised ancestor called Meganeura, found in 300-million-year-old rocks, had a wingspan of over 70 centimetres — the largest insect that has ever lived. Modern dragonflies have changed remarkably little in body plan since those distant days, a testament to how perfectly their design suited the pressures of aerial hunting. When evolution finds a solution this good, it tends not to change it.

Up close, dragonflies are vivid — brilliantly and intensely coloured — flashing metallic blues, emerald greens, fiery oranges, and deep crimsons depending on species. Their enormous compound eyes wrap almost entirely around their heads, giving nearly 360-degree vision and the ability to detect the tiniest movement from metres away. Most remarkably, their four wings operate completely independently of one another, allowing precision manoeuvres no other flying insect can match. Each wing can adjust its angle up to 30 times per second. Scientists studying dragonfly flight have found it so sophisticated that aeronautical engineers are using it as a blueprint for the next generation of drones.

In the air, the dragonfly combines stealthy, silent precision — approaching prey without any warning — with ferocious, explosive speed when it finally strikes. It is one of the most effective predators ever studied: research has found that dragonflies catch their target in over 95% of attempts, a success rate that lions, wolves, and great white sharks cannot approach. Rather than chasing prey directly, dragonflies use a technique called interception flight, calculating where their target will be and flying straight to that point. This kind of predictive thinking requires remarkable processing power for an insect brain.

A dragonfly in pursuit is extraordinarily swift — fast, direct, and accelerating almost instantly from zero to full speed — reaching over 55 kilometres per hour. Yet raw speed is only part of the story. They are also nimble — agile, precise, and able to make tiny mid-air corrections — in a way that seems almost impossible for a creature with four wings and six legs. They can hover motionless for minutes above a single spot, watching and waiting. Their flight inspired the engineers who designed the first practical military drones, and research into dragonfly wing mechanics continues to influence aerospace design today.

Despite being apex aerial hunters — sitting right at the top of the flying insect food chain with no natural predators in the air — dragonflies spend most of their lives hidden underwater. Dragonfly larvae live in ponds and rivers for one to five years, camouflaged in mud and weeds, and are vigilant hunters in their own right — watchful and alert, ambushing fish, tadpoles, and aquatic insects with an extendable lower jaw that shoots forward in milliseconds. The glittering, shimmering adult dragonfly that dazzles us in summer may have lived for years unseen beneath the surface of a muddy pond. Its brief, brilliant adult life is only the final chapter of a much longer story.

insects.title: The Firefly: Living Light in the Dark
insects.blurb: A firefly makes light so efficiently that almost no energy is wasted as heat — a trick that human engineers still cannot replicate — and uses it to write love letters in the dark.
paragraphs: Few sights in the natural world are as magical as a meadow full of fireflies on a warm summer night. These small beetles — for fireflies are not flies at all but beetles — produce their own light, flashing on and off in patterns that fill the darkness with drifting golden sparks. They are found on every continent except Antarctica and Australia, and in parts of Asia and North America they gather in such numbers that entire forests flash in synchrony, thousands of lights pulsing together in perfect time, creating one of the most captivating spectacles in all of nature — so beautiful and mesmerising that it is impossible to look away.

Fireflies evolved the ability to produce their own light independently at least two or three times in different species over the past 100 million years — a remarkable example of the same solution arising separately in response to the same problem. The light is produced inside a special organ in the firefly's abdomen, where a chemical reaction involving a molecule called luciferin releases energy almost entirely as light rather than heat. This makes it extraordinarily luminous and efficient — glowing with almost no waste — in a way that human light bulbs cannot match. A standard light bulb wastes around 90% of its energy as heat; a firefly wastes almost none.

Fireflies are nocturnal — active through the night — and their light is the language they use to find mates. Each species flashes in its own unique pattern: some use long slow pulses, others rapid bursts, others pairs and sequences. A female rests on vegetation and responds to the flash of a passing male with her own signal. These exchanges of radiant light — warm, glowing, and unmistakable — are so species-specific that they act almost like passwords, helping each firefly find a partner of its own kind in the crowded darkness. Some females of certain species even mimic the signals of other species to lure and eat unsuspecting males — a vivid example of evolution's capacity for cunning.

A firefly's glowing light is not only a love signal — it is also a shield. Many fireflies are toxic, carrying bitter or poisonous chemicals absorbed during their larval stage. Their vivid, pulsing lights warn bats, spiders, and frogs that they are unpleasant to eat, in the same way that the bright colours of a dart frog advertise its poison in daylight. This is why the light probably evolved first as a warning system, with courtship communication developing later. Some firefly larvae also glow faintly in the soil, giving them the old name 'glowworms' — an early version of the same chemical system, used purely for defence long before it was used for romance.

Adult fireflies lead an ephemeral existence — brief and fleeting, gone almost as soon as they arrive — with most species living only two to four weeks in their glowing adult form. They do not eat during this time, spending every hour searching for a mate and laying eggs. The real firefly life is underground: larvae live for one to two years in soil or rotting wood, feeding on snails, slugs, and earthworms. In forests and meadows where conditions are right, fireflies become abundant — plentiful and filling the warm air with light — their combined glow bright enough on midsummer nights to cast faint shadows on the grass below.

Firefly populations have fallen in many parts of the world, threatened by light pollution from towns and cities that overwhelms their tiny signals, making it impossible for males and females to find one another. Loss of wetlands and hedgerows removes the moist, sheltered habitats they need. Fireflies are also evasive and difficult to study in detail — quiet, nocturnal, and rarely seen except in flight — making it hard to track exactly how rapidly their numbers are declining. Simple actions, such as turning off unnecessary outdoor lights and leaving patches of long grass undisturbed, can make a genuine difference. A world without fireflies would be a darker place in every sense.

insects.title: The Praying Mantis: The Perfect Ambush Predator
insects.blurb: The praying mantis catches its prey in under 50 milliseconds — faster than the human eye can see — and does so with a success rate that no other hunter on Earth can match.
paragraphs: The praying mantis is one of the most dramatic and distinctive insects in the world. With its triangular head that rotates nearly 180 degrees, its forelegs raised as if in prayer, and its patient, statue-like stillness as it waits for prey, the mantis looks like something designed by an engineer rather than shaped by evolution. There are around 2,500 species worldwide. Some are vivid green; others are patterned like tree bark; a few are so ornate that they resemble orchid flowers in bloom. All of them are highly effective predators. Everything about the praying mantis — from its posture to its eyes to its reflexes — is peculiar and quite unlike any other insect.

Praying mantises evolved around 135 million years ago from an ancestor related to cockroaches and termites. Over millions of years, they developed their iconic raptorial forelegs — hooked, spined, and snapping shut with explosive power — and the unique ability to rotate their heads independently of their bodies, something no other insect can do. The name 'praying' refers to the way the folded forelegs resemble hands held in prayer. The mantis also evolved stereoscopic vision, using both eyes together to judge distance with precision — the only insect known to do so — which is essential for calculating exactly when to launch its lightning strike.

Before the mantis ever strikes, it relies on remaining completely concealed — hidden so well that prey does not even know it exists. Leaf mantises look so exactly like real leaves, complete with fake veins and brown 'damage' spots, that they are almost impossible to spot even a few centimetres away. Bark mantises flatten against tree trunks and vanish. The orchid mantis is pink and white, mimicking a flower petal so convincingly that pollinating insects fly directly toward it expecting food. This extraordinary camouflage is the product of millions of years of selection: the individuals that blended best survived to reproduce, gradually making each generation more convincing. The stealthy approach — quiet, undetectable, and infinitely patient — is the mantis's greatest weapon.

A hunting mantis is perfectly poised — still, balanced, and intensely focused, ready to act in an instant — as it waits for prey to enter range. It is vigilant at every moment, tracking each movement with its swivelling head and large forward-facing eyes that detect even the tiniest shift in position. Then, in a movement lasting just 50 to 70 milliseconds — faster than a human eye can follow — it snaps its forelegs shut, seizing the prey between rows of sharp spines and holding it in a grip that almost nothing can break free from. This strike is one of the fastest predatory movements recorded in the entire insect world.

Once caught, no prey escapes. The mantis is ferocious — powerful, determined, and completely relentless — biting into its victim immediately and eating while it is still alive. Praying mantises eat almost anything they can overpower: grasshoppers, beetles, butterflies, and flies are all regular prey. Larger species have been photographed catching hummingbirds, small lizards, frogs, and even mice. In the wild, the mantis itself must remain nimble — agile and quick-moving — and evasive — difficult to pin down or catch — to escape its own predators, particularly birds and bats that have learned to recognise it despite its camouflage.

The praying mantis is one of the most effective natural pest controllers in gardens and farmland. A single mantis can eat hundreds of harmful insects over a summer, keeping crop-damaging pests in check with no chemicals at all. Many gardeners now actively encourage mantises by planting dense shrubbery and avoiding pesticides that harm them. Despite their ferocious reputation, mantises are entirely harmless to humans and too small to break skin. From the engineering of their strike to the optics of their eyes, the praying mantis stands as one of the most beautifully designed predators in the animal kingdom.

insects.title: The Dung Beetle: Navigator of the Night Sky
insects.habitat: Grasslands and Savannahs of Africa and Asia
insects.blurb: The dung beetle navigates by the Milky Way, was worshipped by ancient Egyptians as a symbol of the sun, and saves farmers billions of pounds a year — all while rolling balls of dung.
paragraphs: The dung beetle has one of the most unusual lifestyles in the insect world — and also one of the most important. These beetles make their living entirely from the droppings of large animals such as elephants, cattle, and zebras. Some roll the dung into balls and push them across the ground to bury elsewhere. Others tunnel straight down beneath a dung pile, dragging portions underground. Others live and breed inside dung permanently. The whole arrangement sounds unpleasant, but it is an extraordinary example of evolution finding exceptional value in something that every other creature abandons. The dung beetle's way of life is, in every sense, peculiar — strange, unexpected, and unlike anything else in the insect world.

Scarab beetles — the family that includes dung beetles — are among the most venerable of all living insects, their lineage stretching back over 65 million years to a world still inhabited by dinosaurs. The ancient Egyptians observed the scarab beetle rolling its ball of dung across the sand and saw in it a symbol of the sun god Ra pushing the sun across the sky. Scarab amulets were worn for luck and carved in gold, and were buried with the dead to guide safe passage to the afterlife. Thousands of years of human reverence were lavished on this small, unassuming beetle, making it one of the most celebrated insects in all of history.

One of the most astonishing recent discoveries in insect science is that dung beetles navigate using the Milky Way. When rolling a dung ball at night, a beetle needs to travel in a straight line away from the pile; if it circles back, rivals will steal its prize. In experiments, scientists fitted tiny cardboard hats onto the beetles' heads to block their view of the sky. The beetles immediately began wandering in circles. Remove the hats and they walked straight lines again. They are the only non-human animals known to navigate by the Milky Way — an ingenious system — clever, resourceful, and quite extraordinary — that nobody suspected until remarkably recently.

Rolling dung demands real dedication. Beetles push balls many times their own body weight across rough, uneven ground, navigating over pebbles, through grass stems, and up slopes without letting go. They are diligent — careful, committed, and thorough — rarely pausing until the ball reaches its destination. Their bodies are robust and compact — solidly built and powerfully muscled — perfectly suited for this heavy labour. Males compete intensely for the best balls, sometimes fighting on top of the dung itself, grappling until one is toppled to the ground. Females inspect the victorious male's ball before deciding whether it meets her standards as a place to lay eggs.

In habitats that might otherwise seem sparse — open, low in nutrients, and difficult to farm — dung beetles provide an ecological service of enormous value. A large dung pile left on the surface kills the grass beneath it. Dung beetles bury it rapidly, enriching the soil with nutrients and improving its ability to retain water. They break the lifecycle of flies and gut parasites that breed in exposed dung, reducing disease in cattle and wild animals. Studies on American cattle ranches have estimated that dung beetles contribute billions of dollars in natural pest control and soil improvement every year. They are tenacious, unstoppable recyclers — determined and always working — that never ask for anything in return.

Dung beetles face an unexpected modern threat: when farmers give livestock antiparasitic drugs, these chemicals remain present and toxic in the animals' dung. Beetles that bury this dung are killed. Combined with the loss of large wild herbivores — elephants, rhinoceroses, and wildebeest — whose dung provides the raw material beetles depend on, some dung beetle species are declining. Yet across their range, these resilient creatures — tough and enduring, shaped by 65 million years of adaptation — continue their essential work in every grassland and savannah on Earth. To protect the dung beetle is, unexpectedly, to protect the health of the entire ecosystem around it.

insects.title: The Ladybird: A Beautiful Warning
insects.blurb: The ladybird's cheerful red spots are a warning to every predator: 'I taste terrible.' Behind its friendly appearance hides one of nature's most sophisticated chemical defence systems.
paragraphs: The ladybird — known as the ladybug in North America — is one of the most loved insects in the world. Bright red or orange with neat black spots, ladybirds are a welcome sight in gardens everywhere, recognised and admired by children and adults alike. There are around 5,000 species worldwide, from the familiar seven-spot ladybird of European gardens to exotic species with yellow and white spots on black backgrounds, blue spots on orange, and every variation in between. But behind that cheerful, harmless appearance lies a sophisticated system of chemical defences refined over millions of years of evolution.

Ladybirds evolved from a family of predatory beetles around 60 million years ago, and their most important evolutionary development was warning colouration. Their vibrant, eye-catching combination of red or orange and black — bold, intense, and impossible to miss — tells any experienced predator: 'I am toxic, and I taste terrible.' This system, called aposematism, works through learning. A young bird that snaps up a ladybird, gets a mouthful of acrid, bitter fluid, spits it out, and feels faintly sick, will never eat another ladybird again. Over millions of generations, the individuals that wore the most vivid, unmistakable warning patterns survived best and passed those colours to their offspring.

When warning colours are not enough, ladybirds have an ingenious backup — clever, reliable, and completely unexpected — called reflexive bleeding. When grabbed by a predator, they ooze small drops of foul-smelling yellow fluid from the joints of their legs, a liquid containing toxic alkaloids that makes them thoroughly unpleasant to eat. Younger ladybirds, whose warning colouration has not yet fully developed, use more concealed strategies — hiding carefully beneath leaves, squeezing into narrow crevices — until their chemical defences are fully operational. Some species also play dead convincingly, dropping from a plant and lying motionless on the ground for several minutes when disturbed.

In spring, ladybirds emerge from winter hiding places — tucked behind bark, under leaf litter, or inside wall crevices — looking for food and mates. Their colour appears radiant in the spring sunlight — warm, glowing, and vivid — after months of hibernation. A female may lay up to a thousand eggs in small batches near aphid colonies, ensuring her larvae hatch with food immediately available. The larvae look nothing like their parents: they are dark, spiny, and elongated. Yet they are equally effective hunters, consuming aphids as efficiently as adults. After a few weeks, each larva forms a pupa attached to a leaf, emerging as a fully formed adult after about a week.

Ladybirds are one of the gardener's greatest natural allies, consuming enormous quantities of aphids, scale insects, and mites that damage plants. A single ladybird can eat up to 5,000 aphids over its lifetime. In years when aphid populations are abundant — plentiful and overwhelming, covering plants in dense clusters — ladybird numbers rise sharply in response, providing natural, chemical-free pest control at exactly the right moment. Ladybirds are also vigilant about their own safety — always alert, reactive to shadows, and quick to withdraw beneath a leaf when a threat appears above them, their antennae constantly sampling the air for chemical warning signals.

Ladybird populations face growing pressure from invasive species, particularly the harlequin ladybird, introduced from Asia to control aphids in greenhouses, which escaped into the wild and now outcompetes native species across Europe and North America. Climate change is also altering the timing of aphid appearances, sometimes leaving newly emerged ladybirds without enough food in early spring. Yet ladybirds have proved resilient across tens of millions of years of environmental upheaval — far tougher than their delicate appearance suggests. Planting aphid-attracting shrubs, leaving fallen leaves undisturbed in autumn, and avoiding broad-spectrum pesticides all help to support these cheerful, spotted beetles in our gardens.

insects.title: The Rhinoceros Beetle: Nature's Strongest Animal
insects.habitat: Tropical Forests of Asia and the Americas
insects.blurb: Relative to its body size, the rhinoceros beetle is the strongest animal on Earth — able to carry over 800 times its own weight — and its extraordinary horns are grown through one of evolution's most dramatic battles.
paragraphs: The rhinoceros beetle is one of the most spectacular insects in the world and, relative to its body weight, the strongest animal on Earth. Males of many species carry enormous horns that branch and curve like a rhinoceros's, giving the insect its memorable name. Rhinoceros beetles are found in tropical forests across Asia, the Americas, Africa, and Australasia, in a stunning variety of forms. The Hercules beetle of Central and South America can exceed 17 centimetres in length — among the biggest insects alive — with a horn extending nearly half its total body length. The Japanese rhinoceros beetle, known locally as the kabutomushi, is one of the most popular pets in Japan, kept and admired by children for its extraordinary appearance.

Rhinoceros beetles belong to the scarab family, which has existed for over 65 million years, but their extraordinary horns are a more recent invention, evolved as weapons specifically for fighting other males. The beetles' bodies are robust — solidly and powerfully constructed — with a thick, armoured outer shell called an elytra that protects delicate flight wings folded inside. This armour is strong enough to deflect the jabs and flips of rival males and resist the beaks and claws of most predators. The horns themselves are made of the same chitin as the rest of the exoskeleton and grow to different sizes depending on the quality of food available during the larval stage: well-fed larvae produce larger horns.

The rhinoceros beetle's strength is almost unbelievable. Scientists have measured beetles carrying immense loads — enormous weights that seem physically impossible for something so small — exceeding 800 times their own body weight without difficulty. The equivalent for an adult human would be carrying about 65 tonnes. Their legs are stout and immovable — thick, compact, and gripping with tremendous force — ending in sharp claws that lock onto bark and branches. This extraordinary strength has a clear evolutionary purpose: the battles between males over mating rights and feeding territories are decided entirely by who can lift, flip, and throw their rival from a branch first.

During breeding season, male rhinoceros beetles are ferocious competitors — aggressive, determined, and unwilling to back down — and watching two males fight for a prime position on a tree trunk is one of the most dramatic spectacles in the insect world. Each male hooks his horn beneath his rival and attempts to lever him upward and throw him to the forest floor. A vigorous male — full of energy and strength, never tiring — will charge repeatedly, trying different angles, wrestling with remarkable stamina for an animal so small. Females observe these contests carefully, choosing the strongest, most successful males as mates, ensuring that their offspring inherit the best possible genes.

Rhinoceros beetles spend most of their lives as larvae — large, creamy white grubs living inside rotting logs or compost, eating decaying wood for one to three years before transforming into adults. This is the period when they do much of their ecological work: breaking down dead wood and releasing its nutrients back into the soil. Adult beetles live only three to six months, feeding on tree sap and fruit. Despite their fearsome appearance, they are entirely harmless to humans — they have no stinger and rarely bite. They are tenacious throughout every stage of their development — determined and steady — pressing forward through each challenge without pausing.

Rhinoceros beetle populations are generally resilient and widespread across tropical forest zones, but deforestation threatens species that depend on specific forest types. In Japan, the kabutomushi is bred commercially as a pet and treated with great care; this cultural affection has kept the species prominent in public awareness. Rhinoceros beetles play a genuine role in their forest ecosystems: larvae break down dead wood, recycling nutrients; adults help disperse fungal spores that maintain healthy forest soil. These extraordinary insects sit at the apex of insect combat — unmatched for strength and spectacle — and remind us that the animal kingdom's most impressive achievements often come packaged in very small bodies.

insects.title: The Locust: When a Grasshopper Transforms
insects.habitat: Grasslands and Farmland of Africa, Asia, and the Middle East
insects.blurb: A locust is not a separate species — it is an ordinary grasshopper that has transformed. One chemical signal from a crowded neighbour can turn a quiet insect into part of a swarm that blackens the sky.
paragraphs: The locust is one of the most destructive natural forces on Earth — and one of the most extraordinary examples of transformation in the animal kingdom. A locust is not a separate species: it is a grasshopper that has changed. Certain grasshopper species are capable of switching between two completely different forms — a solitary form that lives quietly in grassland, and a swarming form that gathers into teeming masses — vast, dense, and crawling with millions of individuals — that devour every plant in their path. This switch, triggered by crowding and chemical signals, is one of the most dramatic behavioural transformations ever discovered in the animal world.

The ability to switch between solitary and swarming forms evolved over millions of years as a response to the unpredictable rainfall patterns of African and Asian grasslands. In good years, heavy rains produce abundant plant growth, and grasshopper populations expand rapidly. When individuals begin crowding together on the remaining plants, physical contact with neighbours triggers a chemical change in the grasshopper's brain. Its colour shifts from camouflage green to warning yellow and black. Its muscles grow stronger. Its brain enlarges. It becomes social, seeking out others of its kind. What was a harmless grasshopper becomes a locust — one of the most remarkable transformations in all of nature.

A locust swarm is one of the most voracious forces in nature — consuming greedily, rapidly, and in almost unimaginable quantities — stripping every leaf, stem, and grain from every plant it passes. A single large swarm can contain 80 billion insects per square kilometre and cover hundreds of square kilometres. The sound of one approaching is described by witnesses as a turbulent, overwhelming roar — churning and chaotic — of millions of beating wings filling the air. In minutes, fields that took months to grow can be stripped down to bare soil, leaving farmers facing total crop failure for the season.

For communities in East Africa, the Middle East, and South Asia, a locust outbreak is a genuine calamity — a disaster of overwhelming scale that threatens the food and livelihoods of millions of people. The 2019 to 2021 Desert Locust outbreak, driven partly by unusually heavy rainfall linked to climate change, swept across 23 countries, threatening the food supply for tens of millions of people in one of the worst humanitarian food crises in decades. The situation was perilous and urgent — dangerous and deeply serious — requiring international responses with aircraft spraying pesticide and thousands of people on the ground working day and night to slow the swarms' advance.

Despite appearing chaotic, locust swarms show remarkable collective intelligence — organised at the group level in ways that no single insect controls or directs. Each locust constantly monitors its nearest neighbours and adjusts its speed and direction in response. The whole swarm navigates toward the most nutritious vegetation, flows around obstacles, and responds to wind and weather. This emergent order — produced by many simple individual rules combining into something far more complex — is similar to the way a flock of starlings moves in perfect formation. Scientists study it to understand how leaderless groups of any kind — from insects to traffic to crowds — can organise themselves so effectively.

Desert locusts can travel up to 150 kilometres in a single day, carried by the wind across vast stretches of land that seem impossible for any individual insect to cross alone. Improved satellite tracking, better weather forecasting, and faster international coordination have made early warning systems more effective in recent decades. Some researchers are developing biological controls using fungi that specifically target locusts without harming other insects. The locust has proved resilient against every eradication effort — tough, adaptable, and impossible to permanently suppress — across millions of years of changing climates. It remains one of the great unresolved challenges of agriculture, and one of nature's most humbling demonstrations of what insects can do when they work together.

insects.title: The Cicada: The 17-Year Sleeper
insects.blurb: The periodical cicada counts 17 years underground in complete darkness before emerging — and does it so precisely that scientists still cannot fully explain how it keeps such perfect time.
paragraphs: The cicada is one of the most astonishing insects in the world, famous above all for its extraordinary lifecycle and the volume of its song. Most people have never seen a cicada, but almost everyone who has spent a summer near woodland knows their sound: a persistent, pulsing roar — ceaseless and rhythmic — that fills the air from dawn until dusk. In parts of the world, a chorus of cicadas can reach 120 decibels, as loud as a rock concert and among the loudest sounds produced by any insect. Yet the most remarkable thing about cicadas is not how loud they are, but how long they wait in silence before making any sound at all.

Cicadas belong to one of the most venerable insect families on Earth — ancient and long-established — with fossils dating back over 250 million years, to a time before the first dinosaurs. There are around 3,000 known species found on every continent except Antarctica. The group that has fascinated scientists most are the periodical cicadas of North America — species that live underground for precisely 13 or 17 years before emerging in enormous synchronised swarms. Indigenous peoples of North America had detailed knowledge of these emergences for thousands of years before European settlers arrived, and their written records describe the same cycles repeating generation after generation with clockwork regularity.

The cicada's underground life is a test of endurance almost without parallel in the insect world. Nymphs — the juvenile stage — hatch from eggs laid in tree branches, fall to the ground, and burrow down to the roots, where they feed on root sap. For 13 or 17 years, they live in complete darkness, slowly and tenaciously growing — determined and patient, underground through droughts and floods and the seasons of the world above — until a precise soil temperature triggers them simultaneously. Then, on one spring night, millions of nymphs across a vast area tunnel to the surface at once, climb whatever vertical object they find, and split open their skins to reveal the adult cicada within.

The emergence of periodical cicadas is one of the great natural spectacles of North America. In the space of a few days, billions of cicadas appear across thousands of square kilometres. Males immediately begin to sing, vibrating a drum-like membrane in their abdomen called a tymbal at up to 500 times per second, producing a thunderous, overwhelming sound — deep, resonant, and relentless — that is one of the defining sounds of the American summer in emergence years. Each female listens for the specific pattern of her own species in the vast chorus, choosing a mate from the billions of singing males surrounding her.

The adult cicada's life is ephemeral — brief and fleeting, measured in weeks rather than the years it spent underground — spent entirely on finding a mate and laying eggs in tree bark. During this time, cicadas face enormous predation from birds, squirrels, raccoons, and foxes. Their survival strategy is to overwhelm predators through sheer numbers: so many emerge simultaneously that every predator eats its fill quickly, yet billions more survive to reproduce before any predator population can grow large enough to make a dent in the swarm. Cicadas are vigilant fliers — reactive and quick to take off when startled — but they rely primarily on abundance rather than camouflage or speed.

Why precisely 13 or 17 years? Scientists believe the prime-number lifespan is an evolutionary strategy to avoid synchronising with the lifecycles of predators or parasites, which operate on two, four, eight, or twelve-year cycles. A predator trying to specialise in cicadas would need to wait 13 or 17 years between meals — long enough that no predator could evolve to depend on them. This strategy has proved resilient — effective and enduring across vast stretches of evolutionary time — surviving ice ages, mass extinctions, and centuries of human land use. After emerging, cicadas enrich the soil as they decompose, aerate the ground with their tunnelling, and feed enormous numbers of other animals. The periodical cicada, with its ancient, precise, and patient schedule, is one of nature's most extraordinary timekeepers.
